title: Form 6B — Affidavit of Service
subtitle: Ontario Family Court — Family Law Rules (FLR 6)
helpIntro: After you serve any court document on the other party, you must file an Affidavit of Service (Form 6B) with the court to prove the documents were delivered. You swear or affirm this form in front of a commissioner of oaths. You need one Form 6B for each time you serve documents.

parts.title: Court information
parts.subtitle: Step 1 of 5
parts.intro: Confirm the court file this service relates to.

fields.label: Courthouse
options: St. Catharines — Superior Court of Justice
Thunder Bay — Superior Court of Justice

fields.label: Court file number
Applicant's full legal name
Respondent's full legal name

parts.title: Who served the documents?
parts.subtitle: Step 2 of 5
parts.intro: The person who physically delivered or sent the documents fills out this section. That can be you, or someone you asked to serve on your behalf (a friend, process server, etc.).

fields.label: Full name of the person who served the documents
fields.helpText: If you served the documents yourself, enter your own name. If someone else served them, enter their name — they will need to sign this affidavit.

fields.label: Age of the person who served the documents
fields.helpText: Must be 18 years of age or older to serve court documents in Ontario.

fields.label: Server's address
fields.helpText: The home or business address of the person who did the serving.

fields.label: City
Province
Postal code

parts.title: Documents that were served
parts.subtitle: Step 3 of 5
parts.intro: List every document you delivered to the other party in this batch of service.

fields.label: List the documents that were served
fields.placeholder:
Form 8 — Application (dated June 1, 2026)
Form 14 — Notice of Motion (dated June 15, 2026)
Form 14A — Affidavit (dated June 15, 2026)
fields.helpText: List each document on a separate line. Include the form number, the document name, and the date on it.

parts.title: How and where the documents were served
parts.subtitle: Step 4 of 5
parts.intro: Describe exactly how you delivered the documents. The method of service affects when the other party is considered to have received them.

fields.label: Full name of the person who was served
fields.helpText: Usually the other party. If they have a lawyer, you may serve the lawyer instead.

fields.label: How were the documents served?
options: Personal service — handed directly to the person
Leaving with adult at residence — left with another adult at their home
Leaving with adult at business — left with person in charge or adult employee
Regular mail — sent by Canada Post
Email — sent by email (with prior agreement or court order)
Acceptance by lawyer — lawyer accepted on their client's behalf

fields.label: Describe the other method of service
fields.placeholder: Describe how the documents were served.

fields.label: Date the documents were served
fields.helpText: The actual date you handed over, mailed, emailed, or couriered the documents.

fields.label: Time the documents were served (if personal service)
fields.helpText: Only required for personal service (hand delivery).

fields.label: Address where service took place
fields.placeholder: e.g. 456 Oak Street, Toronto, ON M4B 1B2
fields.helpText: For mail/email, use the address it was sent to. For personal service, the place where documents were handed over.

fields.label: If you served a person other than the named party, describe who received the documents
fields.placeholder: e.g. A woman who identified herself as the Respondent's spouse, approximately 40 years old.
fields.helpText: For example, if you left documents with an adult at the respondent's home, describe that person.

fields.label: Email address documents were sent to (if served by email)

conditional.showWhen: Email — sent by email (with prior agreement or court order)

fields.label: Did you receive confirmation the email was delivered or opened?

conditional.showWhen: Email — sent by email (with prior agreement or court order)

parts.title: Swearing / commissioning
parts.subtitle: Step 5 of 5
parts.intro: This affidavit must be signed in front of a commissioner of oaths. The commissioner will complete the bottom section. Commissioners are available free at most Ontario courthouses.

fields.label: Will you swear or affirm?
fields.helpText: Both are legally equal. Swearing is a religious oath; affirming is a non-religious promise.

fields.label: Municipality where it will be commissioned
Date it will be commissioned (leave blank to fill at courthouse)
I confirm the service described above took place as stated, and I understand this must be signed in front of a commissioner of oaths.
